title: Conflict-Based Search for Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators
abstract: 本文研究了一种称为多智能体电梯路径规划（MAPF-E）的问题，旨在为多个智能体规划从起点到目标位置的无冲突路径，这些位置可能位于不同楼层，且智能体可通过电梯在楼层间移动。电梯的存在使智能体间的交互复杂化，并为路径规划带来了新的挑战。一方面，由于电梯跨楼层运行时间较长，当大量智能体需前往不同楼层时，极易引发冲突；另一方面，规划器除需考虑智能体位置外，还必须在包含电梯状态的更大状态空间中进行推理。
html_content:
Conflict-Based Search for Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators
Haitong He
1,2
, Xuemian Wu
1
, Shizhe Zhao
1
, Zhongqiang Ren
1†
1
Global College, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, China.
2
Harbin Institute of Technology, China.
†
\dagger
Correspondence: zhongqiang.ren@sjtu.edu.cn
Abstract
This paper investigates a problem called Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators (MAPF-E), which seeks conflict-free paths for multiple agents from their start to goal locations that may locate on different floors, and the agents can use elevators to travel between floors.
The existence of elevators complicates the interaction among the agents and introduces new challenges to the planning.
On the one hand, elevators can cause many conflicts among the agents due to its relatively long traversal time across floors, especially when many agents need to reach a different floor.
On the other hand, the planner has to reason in a larger state space including the states of the elevators, besides the locations of the agents.
To address these challenges, this paper proposes CBS-E, an extension of Conflict-Based Search (CBS) to solve MAPF-E optimally.
CBS-E introduces new concepts of elevator constraints to incorporate the state of the elevator into the conflict resolution process of CBS.
We also adapt and integrate Multi-Valued Decision Diagram (MDD) to CBS-E, referred to as MDD-E.
MDD-E enables intelligently selects conflict to resolve in each iteration, thereby improving the runtime efficiency.
Results show our CBS-E often double or triple the success rates of the baseline, and the proposed conflict reasoning can reduce the number of iterations of CBS-E for up to an order of magnitude.
I
Introduction
Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) seeks conflict-free paths for multiple agents from their respective starts to goals within a shared environment, while minimizing the path cost.
This paper considers a variant of MAPF where the environment consists of multiple floors connected by elevators, and each elevator can transport one agent each time.
The start and goal locations of the agents may lie on different floors, and the agents use elevators to move between floors.
We refer to this variant as Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators (MAPF-E).
When there is only one floor, MAPF-E becomes the regular MAPF.
MAPF is NP-hard
[
17
]
and so is MAPF-E.
MAPF-E arises in logistics when the environment consists of multiple floors.
Consider a 3D warehouse, where mobile robots must use elevators to reach goal locations for retrieving or placing items (Fig.
1
).
Figure 1:
(a) The motivation of MAPF-E. Robots move in a 3D warehouse, while inter-layer transitions are enabled by the elevators (the orange structures). The picture is from ZS Robotics
[
10
]
.
(b) A toy example of MAPF-E, which seeks start-goal paths for the agents. All elevator edge costs are 2, and the black squares represent obstacles. (c) At time
t
=
2
t=2
, agent
i
i
is using the elevator, and agent
j
j
needs to wait at a vertex outside the elevator.
Although a variety of planners for MAPF and its variants were developed, ranging from optimal planners
[
13
,
15
]
, bounded sub-optimal planners
[
1
,
5
]
to unbounded sub-optimal planners
[
7
,
3
]
, we are not aware of any research that focuses on MAPF-E.
A recent work
[
16
]
considers MAPF in a 3D space in which agents can move vertically at arbitrary locations, which is different from the elevator-based setting considered in this work.
Existing approaches for MAPF can be adapted to solve MAPF-E, by describing all floors as a graph with special edges representing the elevator between floors and running a MAPF planner on this graph.
However, such a naive adaption can lead to poor runtime efficiency.
The existence of elevators complicates the interaction among the agents and introduces new challenges into planning.
First, an elevator can couple multiple agents at different layers together, even though these agents are spatially far away from each other, thereby complicating the collision resolution process.
For example, an agent that waits for an elevator to move from floor 8 to 9 would have to wait for another agent inside that elevator that is moving from floor 1 to 2.
Second, it often takes a much longer duration for an elevator to traverse between adjacent layers than an agent moving from one location to another in the same layer, which can lead to potential collision and congestion among the agents frequently, especially when many agents seek to take the same elevator.
In this case, the planner has to consider the possibility of letting an agent take a long detour to use another elevator to optimize the arrival time.
Consequently, the planner must reason over a larger state space that incorporates elevator positions, which burdens the computation.
As a first attempt to study MAPF-E, this paper focuses on developing exact algorithms that can find optimal solutions for MAPF-E.
Based on Conflict-Based Search (CBS)
[
12
]
, a popular approach for MAPF, we develop CBS-E and its variants to optimally solve MAPF-E.
To address the challenges, CBS-E introduces new concepts of elevator conflict and elevator constraints, which incorporates the state of the elevator into the conflict resolution process of CBS to improve runtime efficiency for MAPF-E.
Additionally, we also develop new conflict reasoning techniques by extending Multi-Valued Decision Diagram (MDD), an important data structure for conflict reasoning in CBS
[
11
]
, to MDD with elevator (MDD-E), enabling CBS-E to intelligently select conflicts to resolve in each iteration and further enhance runtime efficiency.
We conduct an ablation study of the proposed conflict reasoning techniques in CBS-E, and compare our CBS-E against a naive adaption of CBS to MAPF-E.
Experimental results show that CBS-E often doubles or triples the success rate of the naive CBS adaption in various maps, and the proposed conflict reasoning techniques can reduce the number of iterations of CBS-E by up to an order of magnitude.

III-A
Conflict-Based Search
Conflict-Based Search (CBS)
[
12

At the high-level, CBS searches by building a binary tree, called the

On Line

IV-A

The conventional conflict resolution technique in CBS can also be applied to resolve elevator conflicts.

This is however inefficient when resolving an elevator conflict since many time steps must be forbidden for an agent.

By contrast, the same elevator conflict can be resolved in a single iteration (Fig.

Remark 1
.
The conventional CBS is known to be inefficient when resolving conflict in long corridor
[
13
]
, a problem that is addressed by
corridor reasoning
[
4
]
.
The key difference between corridor conflicts and elevator conflicts lies in the agents’ behavior: in a feasible solution, agents can wait or avoid other agents by temporarily entering, whereas an elevator can only transport agent between floors and can be unavailable even when no agents are using it (due to resetting).
As a result,
corridor reasoning
is not applicable to elevator conflicts.

cannot be detected, because the nodes in the traditional MDD do not contain elevator-related information and can not capture the state of elevator; (c) shows that based on the MDD-E, the elevator conflict can be successfully detected during the construction of the joint MDD-E.

V-A
Experiment 1: Varying Types of Map
Our first experiment considers two floors connected by 3 elevators, with
t
f
​

3
t_{floor}=3
.
For each map, we run 15 instances with varying numbers of agents
N
N
, and set a runtime limit of 60 seconds for each instance.
In each instance, the start and goal positions of the agents, as well as the locations of the 3 elevators, are randomly generated, ensuring that a valid solution exists.
In these experiments, we compare EC and MDD-E against a naive adaption of CBS to MAPF-E (denoted as CBS).
V-A
1
Success Rates
Fig.
7
(a), (d) show the success rates (i.e., the rate of instances solved by each algorithm within 60 seconds) for the CBS, CBS+EC, CBS+MDD-E, and CBS+EC+MDD-E methods.
Overall, it is evident that incorporating EC into the algorithms often improves the success rate within the same time limit. The introduction of MDD-E alone brings limited improvement and may even perform worse than CBS when
N
≥
11
N\geq 11
. This is because, as the number of agents increases, conflicts become more frequent, and the computational overhead of MDD-E in the conflict selection process restricts the number of conflicts that can be resolved within the time limit.
The computational overhead of MDD-E is often larger for agents with long paths, which is often the case since the agents need to take the elevator to move across multiple floors.
MDD-E demonstrates its advantages when combined with more efficient constraint-handling techniques (e.g., EC).
For instance, on the
random-16-16-10
map, compared with CBS+EC, CBS+EC+MDD-E achieves a higher success rate. This is because, based on EC efficiently resolving conflicts and reducing the repeated occurrence of the same elevator conflicts, MDD-E can further guide the conflict selection process by classifying conflicts, thereby improving the success rate.
However, on the
random-8-8-10
map, this improvement is less pronounced. In particular, when
N
=
14
N=14
, there even exist cases where the success rate of CBS+EC+MDD-E is lower than that of CBS+EC. This is because, with the same number of agents, conflicts are denser on the smaller map and the additional overhead of MDD-E leads to more timeouts. Furthermore, compared with CBS+MDD-E, CBS+EC+MDD-E achieves a much higher success rate due to the more effective constraint-handling method. In fact, on both maps, CBS+EC+MDD-E achieves a success rate that is up to around 40% higher than CBS+MDD-E.
V-A
2
Number of Expansions
Fig.
7

Compared with CBS, CBS+EC resolves elevator conflicts more efficiently, leading to fewer average CT nodes and a higher success rate, as observed in both the
random-8-8-10
and
random-16-16-10
maps.
Furthermore, using MDD-E with Conflict Selection and BP techniques at the high level obviously reduces expansions while ensuring the more critical conflicts are resolved first. The CBS+EC+MDD-E algorithm can solve the same problem with fewer high-level nodes expansions compared to the CBS algorithm. For example, on the
random-16-16-10
map with
N
≥
11
N\geq 11

Since CBS+EC+MDD-E adopts the Conflict Selection technique, it further enlarges the gap in the expansion numbers compared to CBS.

Experiment 3: Varying Number of Floors
Figure 9:
(a): the success rates of CBS, CBS+EC, CBS+MDD-E, and CBS+EC+MDD-E as the number of floors increases, with each floor being the
random-16-16-10

The third experiment investigates the effect of varying the number of floors with 3 elevators, where each floor is based on the
random-16-16-10
map and the number of agents is fixed at
N
=
11
N=11
.
Fig.
9
(a) presents the success rates, showing that both CBS+EC and CBS+EC+MDD-E maintain higher success rates than CBS, and this advantage persists even as the number of floors increases due to the efficient conflict resolution provided by EC. However, as the number of floors increases, the average elevator occupied time per agent also increases, leading to more elevator conflicts and leading to a gradual decline in success rates.
Fig.
9
(b) shows that as the number of floors increases, the rate of time spent on MDD-E and related techniques grows significantly. When the number of floors exceeds 4, this rate in CBS+EC+MDD-E already surpasses 80%, indicating that incorporating MDD-E in such scenarios imposes a substantial overhead on the overall search.
VI
Conclusions and Future Work
This paper investigates a new problem, MAPF-E, and develops efficient methods for resolving elevator conflicts. By using elevator constraints, elevator conflicts can be resolved with only a single expansion while still guaranteeing optimal solutions. By extending the information stored in MDD nodes, we construct a new structure called MDD-E, which serves as the foundation for applying PC and BP techniques. Experimental results demonstrate the advantages of our new approaches under different settings. For future work, one direction is to improve the runtime efficiency when computing MDD-E to speed up CBS-E.
One can also consider planning problems where the elevator has a capacity greater than one.
Finally, it is worthwhile considering non-integer elevator traversal time between floors and combining elevators with MAPF with asynchronous actions
[
9
,
18
]
.

preview_text:
This paper investigates a problem called Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators (MAPF-E), which seeks conflict-free paths for multiple agents from their start to goal locations that may locate on different floors, and the agents can use elevators to travel between floors. The existence of elevators complicates the interaction among the agents and introduces new challenges to the planning. On the one hand, elevators can cause many conflicts among the agents due to its relatively long traversal time across floors, especially when many agents need to reach a different floor. On the other hand, the planner has to reason in a larger state space including the states of the elevators, besides the locations of the agents.

Conflict-Based Search for Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators
Haitong He
1,2
, Xuemian Wu
1
, Shizhe Zhao
1
, Zhongqiang Ren
1†
1
Global College, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, China.
2
Harbin Institute of Technology, China.
†
\dagger
Correspondence: zhongqiang.ren@sjtu.edu.cn
Abstract
This paper investigates a problem called Multi-Agent Path Finding with Elevators (MAPF-E), which seeks conflict-free paths for multiple agents from their start to goal locations that may locate on different floors, and the agents can use elevators to travel between floors.
The existence of elevators complicates the interaction among the agents and introduces new challenges to the planning.
On the one hand, elevators can cause many conflicts among the agents due to its relatively long traversal time across floors, especially when many agents need to reach a different floor.
On the other hand, the planner has to reason in a larger state space including the states of the elevators, besides the locations of the agents.
To address these challenges, this paper proposes CBS-E, an extension of Conflict-Based Search (CBS) to solve MAPF-E optimally.
CBS-E introduces new concepts of elevator constraints to incorporate the state of the elevator into the conflict resolution process
one_line_summary: 这篇论文研究多智能体路径规划问题，涉及电梯使用和冲突解决，与提供的强化学习、视觉语言动作模型等关键词无关。
